perturbation.original_text: 8. Policyholder Rights. Any policyholder holding a policy issued by the Company prior to the termination of this Agreement shall have the right to demand that the Company enforce the Company's rights under paragraphs 1, 3 and 4 of this Agreement, and, if the Company fails or refuses to take timely action to enforce such rights or the Company defaults in any claim or other payment owed to any such policyholder when due, such policyholder may proceed directly against AIG to enforce the Company's rights under paragraphs 1, 3 and 4 of this Agreement; provided, however, that no policyholder of the Company may take any action authorized under this paragraph 8 unless and until (a) such policyholder has given AIG written notice of its intent to enforce the terms of this Agreement as provided in this paragraph 8, which notice shall specify in reasonable detail the nature of and basis for the policyholder's complaint and (b) AIG has failed to comply with this Agreement within sixty (60) days after such notice is given; and, provided, further, that upon termination of this Agreement in accordance with paragraph 7 hereof, the rights of any policyholder as provided for under this paragraph 8 shall terminate effective as of the Termination Date, except with respect to the obligation of AIG (if any) to make capital contributions to the Company pursuant to paragraphs 1, 3 and 4 of this Agreement solely to the extent such obligation arose prior to, and remained unsatisfied as of, the Termination Date (it being understood that upon AIG's satisfaction of all such obligations after the Termination Date, no such policyholder shall have any rights against the Company or AIG, as the case may be, under this paragraph 8).
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Policyholder Rights. Any policyholder holding a policy issued by the Company prior to the termination of this Agreement shall have the right to demand that the Company enforce the Company's rights under paragraphs 1, 3 and 4 of this Agreement, provided, however, that upon termination of this Agreement in accordance with paragraph 7 hereof, the rights of any policyholder as provided for under this paragraph 8 shall terminate effective as of the Termination Date.
perturbation.explanation: By severely limiting the policyholder's ability to enforce AIG's obligations, the contract weakens consumer protection. Specifically, by removing the clause where a policyholder can proceed directly against AIG if the Company defaults, the policyholder's recourse is severely limited. This creates a contradiction with Texas Insurance Code, which requires insurers to provide adequate means for policyholders to receive due compensation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text significantly limits the policyholder's ability to directly enforce the capital maintenance agreement against AIG, contradicting the intent of Texas Insurance Code § 21.21, which aims to protect consumers by ensuring fair claim practices and remedies against insurers. Specifically, the removed section provided a direct avenue for policyholders to seek recourse against AIG if the Company failed to meet its obligations. Removing that clause weakens the policyholder's position and potentially undermines the protections afforded by the Insurance Code.
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits a critical component of policyholder protection by removing the clause that allows a policyholder to proceed directly against AIG if the Company defaults. The Perturbation Explanation confirms that this change weakens consumer protection. The Law Explanation states that Texas Insurance Code § 21.21 seeks to protect consumers by ensuring fair claim practices and remedies against insurers, and that the removed section provided a direct avenue for policyholders to seek recourse against AIG if the Company failed to meet its obligations, thus removing that clause weakens the policyholder's position. The fact that this protection was removed is a clear contradiction according to the provided documentation.

perturbation.original_text: 5. AIG Policies. Subject to the requirements of applicable law and the approval, to the extent required, by any or all of the Company's senior management, relevant management committees, board of directors, and of any insurance regulator, the Company hereby acknowledges that, in a manner consistent with past practice and any other reasonable requirements of AIG, it will comply with all financial and budgetary planning, risk mitigation, derisking or pricing, corporate governance, investment, informational and procedural requirements set forth by AIG.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. AIG Policies. The Company hereby acknowledges that it will comply with all financial and budgetary planning, risk mitigation, derisking or pricing, corporate governance, investment, informational and procedural requirements set forth by AIG.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause "Subject to the requirements of applicable law and the approval, to the extent required, by any or all of the Company's senior management, relevant management committees, board of directors, and of any insurance regulator," the contract now mandates unconditional compliance with AIG's policies, potentially overriding regulatory oversight and the company's governance. This could violate Texas insurance regulations related to corporate governance and internal controls.
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Insurance Code § 82.051 emphasizes the responsibilities of the board of directors in overseeing the insurer's operations and ensuring compliance with regulations. By removing the requirement for compliance to be 'subject to the requirements of applicable law', this modified contract allows AIG to potentially exert control that bypasses the Company's board and regulatory oversight, potentially violating this section of the code.

(b) This chapter also applies to: (1) an agent of an entity described by Subsection (a); and (2) an individual or a corporation, association, partnership, or other artificial person who: (A) is engaged in the business of insurance; (B) holds an authorization; or (C) is regulated by the commissioner. (c) The commissioner's authority under this chapter applies to each form of authorization and each person or entity holding an authorization. Added by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 101, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1999. Amended by Acts 2001, 77th Leg., ch. 1419, Sec. 18, eff. June 1, 2003; Acts 2001, 77th Leg., ch. 1420, Sec. 11.002, eff. Sept. 1, 2001. Amended by: Acts 2007, 80th Leg., R.S., Ch. 730 (H.B. 2636 ), Sec. 2B.010, eff. April 1, 2009. Acts 2017, 85th Leg., R.S., Ch. 967 (S.B. 2065 ), Sec. 5.002, eff. September 1, 2017. Sec. 82.003. PROCEEDINGS UNDER OTHER LAW. The commissioner's authority under this chapter is in addition to any other authority to enforce a sanction, penalty, fine, forfeiture, denial, suspension, or revocation otherwise authorized by law. Added by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 101, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1999. SUBCHAPTER B. IMPOSITION OF SANCTIONS Sec. 82.051. CANCELLATION OR REVOCATION OF AUTHORIZATION. After notice and opportunity for a hearing, the commissioner may cancel or revoke an authorization if the holder of the authorization is found to be in violation of, or to have failed to comply with, this code or a rule of the commissioner. Added by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 101, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1999. Sec. 82.052. OTHER SANCTIONS. In addition to the cancellation or revocation of an authorization under Section 82.051 , the commissioner may: (1) suspend the authorization for a specified time not to exceed one year; (2) order the holder of the authorization to cease and desist from: (A) the activity determined to be in violation of this code or a rule of the commissioner; or (B) the failure to comply with this code or a rule of the commissioner; (3) direct the holder of the authorization to pay an administrative penalty under Chapter 84 ; (4) direct the holder of the authorization to make restitution under Section 82.053 ; or (5) take any combination of those actions. Added by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 101, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1999. Sec. 82.053. RESTITUTION. (a) The commissioner may direct the holder of an authorization to make complete restitution to each Texas resident, each Texas insured, and each entity operating in this state that is harmed by a violation of, or failure to comply with, this code or a rule of the commissioner. (b) The holder of the authorization shall make the restitution in the form and amount and within the period determined by the commissioner. Added by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 101, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1999. Sec. 82.054. CANCELLATION ON FAILURE TO COMPLY. If it is found after hearing that a holder of an authorization has failed to comply with an order issued under Section 82.052 , the commissioner shall cancel each authorization held by the holder. Added by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 101, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1999. Sec. 82.055. INFORMAL DISPOSITION. (a) The commissioner may informally dis
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the clause making compliance with AIG policies 'subject to the requirements of applicable law and the approval' of company management and regulators. The Law Explanation states that Texas Insurance Code § 82.051 emphasizes the board of directors' responsibilities in overseeing the insurer's operations and ensuring compliance with regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Sec. 82.051, states that the commissioner may cancel or revoke an authorization if the holder of the authorization is found to be in violation of, or to have failed to comply with, this code or a rule of the commissioner. By removing the clause subjecting compliance to applicable law and regulatory approval, the contract now mandates unconditional compliance with AIG policies, potentially bypassing regulatory oversight and the company's governance, thus creating a legal contradiction and potentially violating the Texas Insurance Code, as supported by the scraped snippet.

perturbation.original_text: All contributions contemplated under this Agreement shall be approved and made in compliance with applicable law, including, without limitation, approval by the board of directors of each applicable entity and any prior notice or approval requirements specified under applicable rules and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: All contributions contemplated under this Agreement shall be approved by the board of directors of each applicable entity.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "and made in compliance with applicable law, including, without limitation, any prior notice or approval requirements specified under applicable rules and regulations" creates a situation where the contributions might not adhere to Texas state regulations regarding financial transactions of insurance companies. This specifically impacts rules about transparency and external approval of large financial transactions that protect the solvency of the insurance company and protect the policyholder.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text removes the explicit requirement that capital contributions comply with all applicable laws, including those requiring prior notice or approval from regulatory bodies. This creates a potential violation of Texas Insurance Code § 82.053, which grants the Commissioner broad authority to examine and regulate the financial transactions of insurance companies to ensure their solvency and protect policyholders. The omission allows for the possibility of transactions occurring without proper regulatory oversight, thus contradicting the law's intent.

PROCEEDINGS UNDER OTHER LAW. The commissioner's authority under this chapter is in addition to any other authority to enforce a sanction, penalty, fine, forfeiture, denial, suspension, or revocation otherwise authorized by law. Added by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 101, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1999. SUBCHAPTER B. IMPOSITION OF SANCTIONS Sec. 82.051. CANCELLATION OR REVOCATION OF AUTHORIZATION. After notice and opportunity for a hearing, the commissioner may cancel or revoke an authorization if the holder of the authorization is found to be in violation of, or to have failed to comply with, this code or a rule of the commissioner. Added by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 101, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1999. Sec. 82.052. OTHER SANCTIONS. In addition to the cancellation or revocation of an authorization under Section 82.051 , the commissioner may: (1) suspend the authorization for a specified time not to exceed one year; (2) order the holder of the authorization to cease and desist from: (A) the activity determined to be in violation of this code or a rule of the commissioner; or (B) the failure to comply with this code or a rule of the commissioner; (3) direct the holder of the authorization to pay an administrative penalty under Chapter 84 ; (4) direct the holder of the authorization to make restitution under Section 82.053 ; or (5) take any combination of those actions. (a) The commissioner may direct the holder of an authorization to make complete restitution to each Texas resident, each Texas insured, and each entity operating in this state that is harmed by a violation of, or failure to comply with, this code or a rule of the commissioner. Sept. 1, 1999. Sec. 82.054. CANCELLATION ON FAILURE TO COMPLY. If it is found after hearing that a holder of an authorization has failed to comply with an order issued under Section 82.052 , the commissioner shall cancel each authorization held by the holder. Added by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 101, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1999. Sec. 82.055. INFORMAL DISPOSITION. (a) The commissioner may informally dispose of a matter under this subchapter by consent order, agreed settlement, stipulation, or default. (b) An informal disposition may include a provision under which the holder of the authorization agrees to a sanction under this subchapter with the express reservation that: (1) the holder does not admit a violation of this code or of a rule; and (2) the existence of a violation is in dispute. Added by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 101, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1999. Sec. 82.056. NOTICE TO OTHER STATES. The commissioner shall give notice of an action taken under this subchapter to the insurance commissioner or other similar officer of each state. Added by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 101, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1999.
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures compliance with applicable laws, including notice and approval requirements. The changed text omits this crucial requirement, creating a potential legal contradiction. Texas Insurance Code § 82.053 grants the Commissioner authority to regulate financial transactions of insurance companies. By removing the explicit compliance requirement, the modified text allows for transactions that may not be compliant with this code. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly Sec. 82.053 (a), emphasizes the commissioner's power to direct restitution for violations of the code, reinforcing the importance of adherence to the law, which is undermined by the omission in the modified text.
